questions.question:
In a packet switching network, packets are routed from source to destination along a single path having two intermediate nodes. If the message size is M bytes and each packet contains a header of h bytes, then the optimum packet size is:
A. sqrt(Mh/2) + h
B. sqrt(Mh) + h
C. sqrt(2Mh) + h
D. sqrt(Mh/3) + h
questions.to_find: Optimum packet size p (in bytes) that minimizes total transmission time

understand.plain: A message of M bytes is split into packets of size p bytes, each with h bytes of header and (p-h) bytes of data. The path from source to destination has 2 intermediate nodes, meaning 3 links total. Pipelining allows intermediate nodes to forward packets as soon as they arrive. We want the packet size that minimizes total time.

keywords.explain: An intermediate node must receive the entire packet before it can forward it to the next link.
keywords.example: A router buffers a 1000-byte packet fully before sending it out.

keywords.explain: While the source sends the second packet, the first intermediate node forwards the first packet simultaneously, reducing total delay.
keywords.example: With 3 links and 2 packets, the last packet arrives in time proportional to 4 transmission times, not 6.

keywords.explain: The packet size that balances overhead cost (headers per packet) against pipelining benefit (smaller packets pipeline better).
keywords.example: Very small packets waste bandwidth on headers; very large packets reduce pipelining benefit.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='520' height='160' viewBox='0 0 520 160'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='60' width='80' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='50' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Source</text><rect x='150' y='60' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='190' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Node 1</text><rect x='290' y='60' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='330' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Node 2</text><rect x='430' y='60' width='80' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='470' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Dest</text><line x1='90' y1='80' x2='150' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='230' y1='80' x2='290' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='370' y1='80' x2='430' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><text x='120' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Link 1</text><text x='260' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Link 2</text><text x='400' y='70' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Link 3</text><text x='260' y='130' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>3 links, 2 intermediate nodes</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Source connected through 2 intermediate nodes to destination, forming 3 links total in a linear chain.

given.aim: Find the packet size p that minimizes total transmission time over a 3-link pipeline path.

terms.connects: Determines total number of packets = M / (p - h)

terms.meaning: Header size per packet in bytes
terms.connects: Overhead per packet; more packets means more header overhead

terms.meaning: Total packet size (data + header) in bytes
terms.example: e.g., p = 500 bytes total, carrying 480 bytes of data

terms.meaning: 3 links (source to node1, node1 to node2, node2 to destination)
terms.connects: Pipelining saves (number_of_links - 1) * p in time when packets are small

given.plan: Write total transmission time as a function of p. With N = M/(p-h) packets and 3 links, pipelining gives T = N*p + 2*p (in units of p/bandwidth). Minimize T with respect to p by taking dT/dp = 0.

steps.title: Express total transmission time
steps.formula_raw: T = N * p + (L-1) * p where N = M/(p-h), L = 3 links
steps.apply:
Number of packets N = M / (p - h).
With pipelining over L=3 links, total time (ignoring propagation) = N*p + (L-1)*p = [M/(p-h)]*p + 2p.
Let f(p) = M*p/(p-h) + 2p.
steps.note: The (L-1)*p term accounts for the pipeline fill time: after the last packet starts being transmitted, it still needs to traverse 2 more hops.

steps.title: Minimize f(p) by differentiation
steps.note: This is the classic optimum packet size formula for a path with 2 intermediate nodes (3 links).

solution.result: Optimum packet size = sqrt(Mh/2) + h, which is option A.

questions.formula_note: Optimization of pipelining transmission time: differentiate T = M*p/(p-h) + (L-1)*p with respect to p and set to zero, giving p_opt = h + sqrt(M*h/(L-1)).

questions.question:
Consider the store and forward packet switched network given below. Assume that the bandwidth of each link is 10^7 bytes/sec. A user on host A sends a file of size 10^6 bytes to host B through routers R1 and R2 in three different ways. In the first case a single packet containing the complete file is transmitted from A to B. In the second case, the file is split into 10 equal parts and these packets are transmitted from A to B. In the third case, the file is split into 20 equal parts and these packets are sent from A to B. Each packet contains 100 bytes of header information along with the user data. Consider only transmission time and ignore processing, queuing and propagation delays. Also assume there are no errors during transmission. Let T1, T2 and T3 be the times taken to transmit the file in the first, second and third case respectively. Which one of the following is CORRECT?

Network: A -- R1 -- R2 -- B (store-and-forward, each link 10^7 bytes/sec)

A. T1 < T2 < T3
B. T1 > T2 > T3
C. T2 < T3 < T1
D. T1 = T2 = T3
questions.to_find: Ordering of total transmission times T1, T2, T3 for the three packet splitting strategies

understand.plain: A 1MB file is sent from A to B through 2 routers over 3 links. Case 1: sent as one giant packet. Case 2: split into 10 packets. Case 3: split into 20 packets. With store-and-forward and pipelining, smaller packets allow the routers to start forwarding sooner, so we need to compare total transmission time for each strategy.

keywords.explain: Each router must receive the complete packet before forwarding it to the next link.
keywords.example: R1 must receive all 10^6 + 100 bytes before sending to R2 in case 1.

keywords.explain: When sending multiple packets, a downstream router can forward packet 1 while the upstream node sends packet 2, reducing total time.
keywords.example: With 10 packets over 3 hops, total time is much less than 10 times the single-hop time.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='480' height='120' viewBox='0 0 480 120'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='40' width='60' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='40' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A</text><rect x='140' y='40' width='60' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='170' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R1</text><rect x='270' y='40' width='60' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='300' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>R2</text><rect x='400' y='40' width='60' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='430' y='65' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B</text><line x1='70' y1='60' x2='140' y2='60' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='200' y1='60' x2='270' y2='60' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='330' y1='60' x2='400' y2='60' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><text x='105' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>10^7 B/s</text><text x='235' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>10^7 B/s</text><text x='365' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>10^7 B/s</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Linear chain: A -- R1 -- R2 -- B, each link at 10^7 bytes/sec, store-and-forward routing.

given.aim: Determine the ordering of T1, T2, T3 (total file transmission time for 1, 10, 20 packet splits).

terms.connects: Total payload to be delivered to host B

terms.meaning: 100 bytes added to each packet regardless of split
terms.example: For 10 packets: 10 * 100 = 1000 bytes of extra overhead
terms.connects: More packets = more total bytes transmitted

terms.meaning: 10^7 bytes/sec on each of the 3 links
terms.example: Transmitting 10^6 bytes on one link takes 0.1 seconds
terms.connects: Determines transmission time per packet per link

terms.meaning: With N packets over L links, T = N * (packet_size) + (L-1) * (packet_size), in transmission time units
terms.example: More packets = better pipeline fill; savings = (L-1)*(data_per_packet)
terms.connects: More packets give more pipelining gain, reducing T

given.plan: Calculate total bytes transmitted and apply the pipelining formula T = N*p + (L-1)*p for each case. Compare the three totals.

steps.title: Case 1: Single packet (N=1)
steps.apply:
Packet size p1 = 10^6 + 100 = 1,000,100 bytes.
With pipelining and N=1: T1 = 3 * p1 / 10^7 (all 3 links used sequentially).
T1 = 3 * 1,000,100 / 10^7 = 0.30003 seconds.
steps.note: With a single packet there is no pipelining benefit; it must traverse each link one at a time.

steps.title: Case 2: 10 packets (N=10)
steps.note: Pipelining benefit is significant: instead of 3 * 10 transmissions, only 12 are needed in the critical path.

steps.title: Case 3: 20 packets (N=20)
steps.note: T3 < T2 because more packets = better pipelining, despite slightly more header overhead.

steps.title: Compare T1, T2, T3
steps.note: More packet splits always improve time when the pipelining gain outweighs header overhead, which is the case here since header (100B) << data per packet.

solution.result: T1 > T2 > T3, which is option B.

questions.formula_note: Store-and-forward pipelining: total time = (N + L - 1) * p / bandwidth, where N = number of packets, L = number of links, p = packet size.

questions.question: Two hosts are connected via a packet switch with 10^6 bits per second links. Each link has a propagation delay of 20 microseconds. The switch begins forwarding a packet 35 microseconds after it begins receiving the same. If 10000 bits of data are to be transmitted between the two hosts using a packet size of 5000 bits, the time elapsed between the start of transmission of the first bit of the first packet at the sender and the receipt of the last bit of the last packet at the receiver is _________ microseconds.
questions.to_find: Total time in microseconds from first bit sent to last bit received at destination

understand.plain: Two hosts are connected through one packet switch (one intermediate node, two links). Data of 10000 bits is split into 2 packets of 5000 bits each. Each link is 10^6 bps, propagation delay is 20 microseconds per link, and the switch adds a 35-microsecond processing/buffering delay before starting to forward each packet.

keywords.explain: Time to push all bits of a packet onto the link = packet_size / link_bandwidth.
Time for signal to travel from one end of a link to the other (physical distance).
The switch starts forwarding 35 microseconds after it BEGINS receiving (not after fully receiving). This is a cut-through-like timing but with 35 us latency.
keywords.example: If packet arrives at t=0, the switch starts forwarding at t=35 us

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='500' height='140' viewBox='0 0 500 140'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='50' width='80' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='60' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Host A</text><rect x='210' y='50' width='80' height='40' class='bg'/><text x='250' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Switch</text><rect x='400' y='50' width='80' height='40' class='hi'/><text x='440' y='75' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Host B</text><line x1='100' y1='70' x2='210' y2='70' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><line x1='290' y1='70' x2='400' y2='70' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='3'/><text x='155' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>10^6 bps</text><text x='345' y='50' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>10^6 bps</text><text x='155' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>prop=20us</text><text x='345' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>prop=20us</text><text x='250' y='115' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Switch delay: 35us after start of receive</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Host A connected to packet switch and switch connected to Host B, each link at 10^6 bps with 20 us propagation delay. Switch begins forwarding 35 us after starting to receive.

given.aim: Compute total end-to-end delay for 10000 bits (2 packets of 5000 bits) from first bit sent to last bit received.

terms.meaning: 5000 bits per packet; total data = 10000 bits = 2 packets
terms.connects: Determines transmission time per packet = 5000/10^6 = 5000 us

terms.example: First bit sent at t=0 arrives at switch at t=20 us

terms.meaning: Switch starts forwarding 35 us after it BEGINS receiving a packet (not after full receipt)
terms.example: Switch starts receiving packet at t=20; starts forwarding at t=20+35=55 us
terms.connects: This is the key timing for when the second link transmission starts

given.plan: Trace the timeline for both packets. Calculate when the last bit of the last packet arrives at Host B, then subtract 0 (start time) to get elapsed time.

steps.title: Transmission times and basic delays
steps.apply:
Transmission delay per packet = 5000 bits / 10^6 bps = 5000 microseconds.
Propagation delay per link = 20 us.
Switch forwarding delay = 35 us after START of receiving.
steps.note: The switch begins forwarding 35 us after it STARTS receiving (not after it finishes). So it starts forwarding at: time_first_bit_arrives + 35 us.

steps.title: Track Packet 1 through the network
steps.apply:
t=0: Host A starts transmitting packet 1.
t=5000: Host A finishes transmitting packet 1 (last bit leaves Host A).
t=0+20=20 us: First bit of packet 1 arrives at switch (after propagation).
t=20+35=55 us: Switch begins forwarding packet 1 onto link 2.
t=55+5000=5055 us: Switch finishes transmitting packet 1.
t=5055+20=5075 us: Last bit of packet 1 arrives at Host B.
steps.note: The switch starts forwarding before it even finishes receiving packet 1, since 35 < 5000 us.

steps.title: Track Packet 2 through the network
steps.apply:
t=5000 us: Host A starts transmitting packet 2.
t=5000+20=5020 us: First bit of packet 2 arrives at switch.
t=5020+35=5055 us: Switch begins forwarding packet 2. But wait - switch was still sending packet 1 until t=5055. So packet 2 forwarding can begin at max(5055, 5055) = 5055 us... actually switch finishes sending packet 1 at t=5055. And it starts ready to forward packet 2 at t=5055. So packet 2 forwarding starts at t=5055 us.
t=5055+5000=10055 us: Switch finishes transmitting packet 2.
t=10055+20=10075 us: Last bit of packet 2 arrives at Host B.
steps.note: Note: switch needs to finish forwarding packet 1 (ends at 5055) before forwarding packet 2. The switch is ready to forward packet 2 at 5055 (same time), so no extra wait. Total elapsed = 10075 us. But let me re-examine: switch starts forwarding P2 at t = max(when P1 done on link2, when switch ready for P2) = max(5055, 5055) = 5055. Yes, 10075 us.

steps.title: Verify: check if switch is free when packet 2 is ready
steps.apply:
Switch ready to forward P2: first bit of P2 arrives at switch at t=5020, plus 35 us delay = t=5055.
Switch finishes forwarding P1: t=5055.
So switch starts P2 exactly at t=5055 (no queuing delay).
Last bit of P2 arrives at Host B: 5055 + 5000 + 20 = 10075 us.
Elapsed time = 10075 - 0 = 10075 us.

However, re-reading: the standard GATE 2015 answer is 5095 us. Let me re-examine the problem parameters.

Actual GATE 2015 problem: links are 10^6 bps, propagation delay 20 us, switch delay 35 us after BEGINNING to receive, data = 10000 bits, packet size = 5000 bits.

For packet 1:
- t=0: A starts sending P1
- First bit of P1 reaches switch: t = 20 us (propagation)
- Switch starts forwarding: t = 20 + 35 = 55 us
- Last bit of P1 leaves A: t = 5000 us
- Last bit of P1 reaches switch: t = 5000 + 20 = 5020 us
- Switch finishes forwarding P1: t = 55 + 5000 = 5055 us
- Last bit of P1 reaches B: t = 5055 + 20 = 5075 us

For packet 2:
- t=5000: A starts sending P2
- First bit of P2 reaches switch: t = 5020 us
- Switch would start forwarding P2: t = 5020 + 35 = 5055 us
- Switch is busy with P1 until t=5055, so P2 starts at t=5055
- Last bit of P2 leaves switch: t = 5055 + 5000 = 10055 us
- Last bit of P2 reaches B: t = 10055 + 20 = 10075 us

Total = 10075 us. But the accepted GATE answer is 5095 us.

Let me reconsider: perhaps the problem means packet size is 5000 bits but total data is 10000 bits EXCLUDING headers, and the question says 'packet size of 5000 bits' means 1 packet only (since 10000/5000 = 2, but maybe 1 packet of 5000 and we need only to send... no).

Actually re-reading the image carefully: the problem says '10000 bits' and 'packet size of 5000 bits'. That is 2 packets. The link is 10^6 bps.

Wait - I need to re-read the actual image values more carefully.
steps.note: The official GATE 2015 answer for this question is 5095 microseconds. This implies the data might be transmitted as a single 5000-bit packet with the 10000 bits being something else, or the parameters differ slightly from the OCR-mangled hint.

steps.title: Re-examine with GATE official answer: single packet scenario
steps.formula_raw: T = t_tx1 + t_prop1 + t_switch + t_tx2 + t_prop2
steps.apply:
If there is 1 packet of 5000 bits (perhaps 10000 bits total but the path has different parameters):
Actually the correct GATE 2015 CS question has: 10^6 bps links, prop delay 20 us, switch delay 35 us, 10000 bits data, packet size 5000 bits (2 packets).

The answer 5095 us arises if:
- t_tx = 5000 / 10^6 = 5000 us per packet... that gives ~10075 not 5095.

Unless links are 10^9 bps (1 Gbps):
- t_tx = 5000 / 10^9 = 5 us
- P1: start t=0, first bit reaches switch at t=20, switch starts fwding at t=55, P1 done at t=5, last bit reaches switch at t=25, switch done fwding P1 at t=55+5=60, reaches B at t=80
- P2: start t=5, first bit reaches switch t=25, switch ready at t=25+35=60, switch sends P2 from t=60 to t=65, last bit reaches B at t=65+20=85
Still not 5095.

Let me try bandwidth = 10^6 bps and packet size = 5000 bits, 2 packets:
Actual timeline gives 10075. The answer 5095 may come from a different version of the question.

With the answer being 5095: T = 5*1000 + 20 + 35 + 20 + 20 = 5095 where 5*1000=5000 us (one packet tx on link1), prop=20, switch=35, tx on link2=5000... wait 5000+20+35+5000+20=10075 not 5095.

5095 = 5000 + 20 + 35 + 20 + 20? That's 5095. If the switch transmits in 35 us flat and link2 tx is also 20 us... No.

5095 = 5000 (tx on link 1) + 20 (prop link1) + 35 (switch delay) + 20 (prop link2) + 20 (??)
Or: 5095 = 5000 + 75 + 20 = 5095. Where 75 = 20+35+20.
So: tx_p1=5000, prop1=20, switch=35, prop2=20, last_bit=20? 
This makes sense if there is ONLY ONE PACKET:
- t=0: start sending (5000 bits)
- t=5000: finish transmitting
- last bit arrives at switch: t=5000+20=5020
- switch starts forwarding at t = 0+35=35, but must wait until it receives the full packet. Actually: 'begins forwarding 35 us after it receives' means 35 us after the packet is FULLY received.
- So switch starts forwarding at t = 5020 + 35 = 5055
- Switch finishes: t = 5055 + 5000 = 10055
- Last bit at B: t = 10055 + 20 = 10075

Alternatively '35 us after it BEGINS receiving': switch starts at t = 20+35=55; done at 55+5000=5055; arrives B at 5055+20=5075.

For 2 packets: answer is 5075 + (5000 + 5000 + 20) wait that doesn't work...

Given official answer 5095, the calculation must be:
5095 = 5000 (P1 on link1) + 20 (prop1) + 35 (switch) + 5000 (P1+P2 together on link2... no)

Perhaps: only 1 packet is sent (5000 bits), and data is 5000 bits not 10000?
T = 5000 + 20 + 35 + 5000 + 20 = 10075 for 1 packet. Still not 5095.

Or perhaps: links are 10^8 bps, packet=5000 bits:
tx = 5000/10^8 = 50 us
T(2 pkt) = 50+20+35+50+20 + 50+20 = Hmm.

Let me try with the exact answer working backward:
5095 = a + 20 + 35 + a + 20 + 20? 
5095 = 2a + 95 => a = 2500, so tx = 2500 us => 2500 bits at 10^6 bps. Doesn't match.

5095 = a + 20 + 35 + a + 20: 5095 = 2a + 75 => a=2510. Doesn't fit.

5095 for 2 packets of 5000 bits:
P1 sent 0 to 5000, arrives switch 20 to 5020.
Switch starts forwarding at 55 (35 after start of receiving).
Switch sends P1 on link2: 55 to 5055.
P1 last bit at B: 5075.
P2 sent 5000 to 10000; first bit arrives switch at 5020, switch delay: 5055, but link2 busy till 5055. So P2 starts at 5055, ends 10055, arrives B: 10075.
Answer: 10075 us.

But GATE official answer = 5095. This means total data must be only 5000 bits (1 packet)... but question says 10000 bits.

Alternatively, perhaps the switch delay of 35 us already INCLUDES the transmission time for 35 bits (at 10^6 bps = 35 bits in 35 us). So the switch starts forwarding when it has received 35 bits (header?). This is cut-through switching. In that case:
- Switch starts forwarding P1 at t = 20 + 35 = 55 us
- Switch finishes forwarding P1 at t = 55 + 5000 = 5055 us  
- P1 last bit at B: 5075 us
- P2 starts at t = 5000 us (A starts sending)
- P2 first bit arrives switch at 5020; switch starts at 5055 (max of 5055 from P2 processing and link free at 5055). Tied.
- P2 last bit at B = 5055 + 5000 + 20 = 10075 us.

I'm getting 10075 consistently. The GATE 2015 official key might have 5095 for a slightly different set of parameters. Going with 10075 as the calculated answer.
steps.note: Based on the given parameters (10^6 bps, 20 us propagation, 35 us switch delay after start of receive, 10000 bits, 5000-bit packets), the calculated answer is 10075 microseconds. However, if the official answer is 5095, the problem likely had different parameters (possibly only 1 packet or higher bandwidth).

solution.result: 10075 microseconds (based on 2 packets of 5000 bits each over 10^6 bps links with 20 us propagation and 35 us switch delay after start of receive). The time elapsed is 10075 microseconds.

questions.formula_note: End-to-end delay = sum of transmission delays + propagation delays + switch processing delays, traced packet by packet through the pipeline.

questions.question:
Which one of the following statements is FALSE?
A. Packet switching leads to better utilization of bandwidth resources than circuit switching
B. Packet switching results in less variation in delay than circuit switching
C. Packet switching requires more per-packet processing than circuit switching
D. Packet switching can lead to reordering unlike circuit switching
questions.answer: B. Packet switching results in less variation in delay than circuit switching
questions.to_find: Which statement about packet switching vs circuit switching is FALSE

understand.plain: This question tests knowledge of the differences between packet switching and circuit switching. Three statements should be true and one false. Packet switching shares links among many users, leading to variable delays due to queuing, while circuit switching dedicates a path so delay is constant.

keywords.explain: Data is broken into packets that each independently find their way through the network, sharing links with other traffic.
keywords.example: The Internet uses packet switching; packets may take different routes and arrive out of order.

keywords.explain: A dedicated end-to-end path is established before communication begins; resources are reserved for the entire call duration.
keywords.example: Traditional telephone (PSTN) uses circuit switching; you get a guaranteed, dedicated channel.

keywords.explain: Variation in packet delay, caused by queuing at routers in packet-switched networks.
keywords.example: In VoIP over the Internet, packets sometimes arrive late due to jitter, causing choppy audio.

understand.visual_alt: Comparison table: packet switching vs circuit switching - bandwidth utilization, delay variation, per-packet processing, and ordering.

given.aim: Identify the false statement among the four claims about packet vs circuit switching.

terms.meaning: How efficiently the link bandwidth is used by traffic
terms.example: Circuit switching wastes bandwidth during silence; packet switching uses it for other traffic
terms.connects: Statement A claims packet switching is better here - this is TRUE

terms.meaning: How much the delay fluctuates from packet to packet
terms.example: In packet switching, queuing means some packets wait longer than others
terms.connects: Statement B claims packet switching has LESS variation - this needs evaluation

terms.meaning: Work done at each node for each packet: routing table lookup, header inspection, etc.
terms.example: In circuit switching, once the circuit is set up, no per-packet decisions are needed
terms.connects: Statement C claims packet switching needs more processing - this is TRUE

terms.meaning: Packets may arrive out of order if they take different paths
terms.example: In circuit switching, all data follows the same dedicated path, so order is preserved
terms.connects: Statement D claims packet switching can reorder unlike circuit switching - this is TRUE

given.plan: Evaluate each statement against known properties of packet vs circuit switching. The FALSE statement is B, because packet switching actually has MORE delay variation (jitter) than circuit switching, not less.

steps.title: Evaluate statement A: bandwidth utilization
steps.apply: Packet switching is statistical multiplexing - multiple users share links dynamically. If one user is idle, others can use the full bandwidth. Circuit switching reserves bandwidth for each circuit even when idle. Therefore, packet switching leads to BETTER bandwidth utilization. Statement A is TRUE.
steps.note: This is one of the main advantages of packet switching over circuit switching.

steps.title: Evaluate statement B: delay variation
steps.apply: In packet switching, packets queue at each router. Queue lengths vary with traffic load, causing variable queuing delay. Some packets wait longer, some shorter. This leads to HIGH delay variation (jitter). In circuit switching, the dedicated path has fixed, predictable delay with NO queuing variation. Therefore, packet switching results in MORE (not less) variation in delay. Statement B is FALSE.
steps.note: Jitter in packet-switched networks is a well-known problem for real-time applications like voice and video.

steps.title: Evaluate statements C and D to confirm
steps.apply:
C: Circuit switching only needs setup once; each intermediate switch just passes bits through the established circuit. Packet switching requires each router to look up routing tables and make forwarding decisions for every packet. Statement C is TRUE.
D: In packet switching, different packets of the same message may take different routes, arriving out of order. In circuit switching, all data follows the single established circuit in order. Statement D is TRUE.
steps.note: All of A, C, D are true; only B is false, making B the correct answer.

solution.result: Statement B is FALSE. Packet switching results in MORE (not less) variation in delay compared to circuit switching, due to queuing at intermediate routers.

questions.formula_note: Packet switching vs circuit switching: key difference is that packet switching uses statistical multiplexing causing variable queuing delay (jitter), while circuit switching provides constant dedicated-path delay with no jitter.
